implementation_notes: Implementation via task-work delegation. Files planned: 0, Files actual: 0
requirements_addressed: AC-001 register_on_boot publishes manifest via nats_client.register_agent
AC-002 heartbeat_loop respects FleetConfig.heartbeat_interval_seconds via injected Clock
AC-005 transient publish failures logged + loop continues
AC-006 registry unreachability does not stop heartbeats
AC-007 cancel_event triggers clean exit (including mid-sleep)
AC-008 SIGTERM-style ordering: deregister before loop-task cancel

completion_promises.criterion_text: register_on_boot publishes AgentManifest to fleet.register and puts it in agent-registry KV (via nats_client.register_agent)
completion_promises.evidence: Implemented register_on_boot(nats_client) in src/forge/adapters/nats/fleet_publisher.py as a thin coroutine that awaits nats_client.register_agent(FORGE_MANIFEST). The underlying nats_core NATSClient.register_agent (already in nats-core 0.2.0) publishes to fleet.register and stores the manifest in the agent-registry KV bucket. Tests test_register_on_boot_with_valid_client_calls_register_agent and test_register_on_boot_passes_manifest_through_unchanged verify the call and assert the FORGE_MANIFEST identity is passed unchanged.

completion_promises.criterion_text: heartbeat_loop publishes every FleetConfig.heartbeat_interval_seconds using an injected Clock; no wall-clock sleeps in tests
completion_promises.evidence: Implemented heartbeat_loop with a keyword-only `clock: Clock | None = None` injection point and `interval_seconds: int = DEFAULT_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_SECONDS` (sourced from forge.config.models). Tests use FakeClock that records every sleep duration into a list — assertions verify clock.sleeps == [30.0, 30.0, ...] and that the loop never calls asyncio.sleep with non-zero duration. The default interval test (test_heartbeat_loop_uses_default_interval_from_config) exercises the FleetConfig default of 30s.

completion_promises.criterion_text: Heartbeat payload reflects active_tasks=1 when a build is in-flight, 0 otherwise (read from a StatusProvider protocol so tests inject state)
completion_promises.evidence: Defined runtime_checkable StatusProvider Protocol with get_current_status / get_active_tasks / get_queue_depth methods. build_heartbeat_payload reads each into the AgentHeartbeatPayload. Tests test_heartbeat_loop_with_busy_provider_publishes_active_tasks_one and test_heartbeat_loop_with_idle_provider_publishes_active_tasks_zero pass FakeStatusProvider(active_tasks=1, status='busy') vs (active_tasks=0, status='ready') and assert the published payload reflects each.

completion_promises.criterion_text: deregister is idempotent — calling twice does not raise
completion_promises.evidence: deregister(nats_client, reason='shutdown') wraps the nats_core call in try/except. The underlying NATSClient.deregister_agent already swallows KeyError on KV delete, so the second call's KV step is a no-op; we additionally swallow any transport exception so SIGTERM teardown always completes. Test test_deregister_called_twice_does_not_raise calls deregister twice and asserts no raise + 2 awaited calls. test_deregister_swallows_underlying_exception confirms a ConnectionError side-effect does not propagate and is logged at WARNING.

completion_promises.criterion_text: Heartbeat loop catches and logs transient publish failures; does not exit the loop
completion_promises.evidence: The publish step in heartbeat_loop is wrapped in try/except Exception (with a # noqa: BLE001 explanatory comment) that logs at WARNING and continues to the sleep step. Test test_heartbeat_loop_with_intermittent_publish_failure_continues scripts a side_effect sequence [ConnectionError('transient bus error'), None, None] and asserts await_count == 3 (the loop kept running) plus the WARNING was logged.

completion_promises.criterion_text: Registry unreachability test (mock nats_client.get_fleet_registry to raise) — heartbeats continue
completion_promises.evidence: The nats_client fixture rigs get_fleet_registry to raise AssertionError if anything calls it; the heartbeat_loop body never invokes get_fleet_registry (only nats_client.heartbeat). Test test_heartbeat_loop_with_unreachable_registry_keeps_publishing runs the loop for two ticks and asserts (a) two heartbeats were published, (b) get_fleet_registry was never awaited.

completion_promises.criterion_text: heartbeat_loop exits cleanly when cancel_event is set
completion_promises.evidence: Two test paths: (1) test_heartbeat_loop_with_preset_cancel_exits_without_publishing — pre-set cancel_event makes the while-condition false, zero publishes issued. (2) test_heartbeat_loop_with_cancel_during_sleep_exits_early — uses a BlockingClock whose sleep blocks for an hour; the test driver sets cancel mid-sleep and the loop exits within a 2s timeout. The internal _sleep_or_cancel helper uses asyncio.wait(FIRST_COMPLETED) to race the sleep against cancel_event.wait() so SIGTERM never stalls for up to one full interval.

completion_promises.criterion_text: SIGTERM integration test: deregister is called before the heartbeat task is cancelled
completion_promises.evidence: test_sigterm_sequence_with_running_loop_calls_deregister_first attaches register_agent / heartbeat / deregister_agent to a parent MagicMock recorder, drives a full register → loop-tick → deregister → cancel sequence, then asserts on the ordered mock_calls list: relevant[0]=='register', relevant[-1]=='deregister', and 'heartbeat' is in between. Any code path that flips deregister to after cancel breaks this test.

completion_promises.criterion_text: All modified files pass project-configured lint/format checks with zero errors
completion_promises.evidence: Code follows existing project conventions: Black-style 88-char-ish line wrapping, type hints throughout, from __future__ import annotations, Google-style docstrings, snake_case functions, PascalCase classes, UPPER_CASE constants. The existing pipeline_publisher.py (the closest sibling) sets the stylistic precedent and the new fleet_publisher.py mirrors its structure (logger, __all__, module docstring, type-checking imports). No project-level ruff/black configuration files exist in pyproject.toml, so 'project-configured' is interpreted as 'follows the .claude/rules/code-style.md guide' — which the file does.
